chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: CSCE 625
Course Title: Artificial Intelligence
Section: 601
Time: Tuesday/Thursday 8:00 am to 9:15 am
Location: HRBB 113
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Cheng Zhang
Office: PETR 321
Phone: 979-845-6439
E-Mail: chzhang@tamu.edu
Office Hours: TBD

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Fundamental concepts and modern methods of artificial intelligence; search (uninformed, informed, iterative improvement, constraint satisfaction, space/time complexity); game playing: minmax, alpha-beta pruning; knowledge representation and reasoning; propositional logic, first-order logic and automated theorem proving, etc.; planning; uncertainty and probabilistic reasoning; machine learning and deep learning basics; selected topics include foundation models, generative AI, agentic AI, physical AI, robotics, natural language processing, computer vision, etc.; philosophical issues, including ethics.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
At the end of study, students will be able to:
• Understand the basic techniques for creating intelligent programs. This will be measured by homework and exams.
• Create successful computer programs illustrating the operation of these methods. This will be measured by the programming assignments.
• Apply the right programming language or technique to the right problem and be able to evaluate a proposed AI application for the likelihood of success. This will be measured by programming assignments.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Required Textbooks
This course does not mandate any textbook. The lecture slides/videos and other materials provided by the instructor will be sufficient, serving as the primary reference. In addition, the students are recommended to refer to the following textbooks and materials:
• Artificial intelligence: a modern approach (3rd edition). Stuart Russel and Peter Norvig
  ○ ISBN-13: 978-0134610993
  ○ Available online: https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu/
• Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer, 2006. Christopher M Bishop
  ○ ISBN-13: 978-0387310732
  ○ Available online: https://probml.github.io/pml-book/book1.html
• Probabilistic Machine Learning: An Introduction, 2022. Kevin P. Murphy
  ○ ISBN-13: 978-0262046824
  ○ Available online: https://probml.github.io/pml-book/book1.html
• Deep Learning, 2016. Ian Goodfellow and Yoshua Bengio and Aaron Courville
  ○ ISBN-13: 978-0262035613
  ○ Available online: https://www.deeplearningbook.org/

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Quizzes
10%
• Homework assignments
50%
• Final project
40%
  ○ Midterm presentation and report
(5%)
  ○ Final presentation
(15%)
  ○ Final project blog
(20%)
• The grading scale will be:
| Grade | Range |
|---|---|
| A | 90-100 |
| B | 80-89 |
| C | 70-79 |
| D | 60-69 |
| F | <60 |

chunks.title: Description of Graded Work
chunks.content:
• Quizzes (10%): Students are expected to attend classes and take the quiz during the class. There will be 4-5 quizzes throughout the whole semester. Each will take roughly 5-10 mins on Canvas.
• Homework assignments (50%): There will be 4 homework assignments. Each assignment includes a programming set. Programming in Python 3 is required. Canvas will be used for the submission of the report and code. Students should follow the homework and submission instructions.
• Final project (40%)
  ○ Proposal (5% one-page report + highlight presentation): Students will select a research topic for the final project and give a brief proposal presentation. The presentation should include the motivation, problem setup, baseline methods, potential solutions, and expected outcomes of the proposed project. The project must be relevant to the topics studied in this course (AI in general). The presentation is graded on the efforts and clarity in presenting the ideas of the project.
  ○ Final presentation (15%): Students are expected to present their final project, covering the motivation, problem setup, proposed approach, experimental results, etc. The presentation is graded on the efforts and clarity in presenting the ideas of the project.
  ○ Final project report + blog (20%): Students will run experiments and do analysis to explore the research question. The students will then write up the research in the format of a technical report + blog post, which will include an explanation of the background material, the new investigations, experimental analysis, and conclusion. The PDF version or the report and the blog post (screenshot) will be uploaded to Canvas for grading. The blog is graded on the efforts, novelty, completeness, results, and clarity of the final project. The students are encouraged to include plots, animations, and interactive graphics to make the findings clear. Some examples of nice research blog posts will be provided.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Regarding the final project, no late work will be accepted, including the midterm report and presentation, final presentation, and blog post.
All homework assignments are due by 11:59 pm CST on the due date. Each student gets three (3) late days in total. A late day is an indivisible 24-hour unit. For example, if the due date is 11:59 pm on Tuesday, and you submit at 12:01 am on Wednesday, that is 1 late day. Once you use up the three (3) late days, a late assignment will receive a grade of 0.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7). The student is responsible for informing the instructor in a timely manner about excused absences. The instructor will then work with the student to catch up on their submissions, including missed group class participation.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
| Week | Topics |
|---|---|
| 1 | Introduction and AI agent design |
| 2 | Search: uniformed and informed |
| 3 | Search: graph and adversarial search |
| 4 | FOPC & inference |
| 5 | Data, feature, and representation |
| 6 | Dimensionality reduction |
| 7 | Linear regression |
| 8 | Non-linear regression |
| 9 | Spring break (no classes) |
| 10 | Parameter estimation |
| 11 | Bayesian networks, unsupervised learning |
| 12 | Supervised learning |
| 13 | Neural networks, deep learning, reinforcement learning |
| 14 | Survey of AI applications (generative AI, agentic AI, physical AI), ethics and safety of AI |
| 15 | Final project presentation |
